That takes us to where we are today, making the move we did to roll the trade and then writing another round of covered calls.

With the expiration coming up this week, and shares of SCHD rising since we entered a short put position previously, we can close these latest puts out for a minimal amount and write another round. We originally collected a $0.28 premium, with the $0.03 closing cost, we are realizing around 89.3% of the expected profit.

However, to get a more reasonable premium, we are having to roll up to the $27 strike price. This is now a trend we've been moving on as the price has been rising.

Yesterday, shares were about flat at the immediate open. (At the time of writing)

That said, instead of simply doing a roll trade here, I'm moving into a short strangle position by writing covered calls at the $28 strike price. As a reminder, this is where we were previously assigned short puts, and as the price has risen, it is allowing us to once again return to writing some covered calls.

The next ex-distribution date is coming up on September 2024; with that, I kicked out the expiration date to October instead of the September expiration. If the shares are in-the-money, it is possible to have the position called early, but it is less likely with a later expiration date.

Therefore, while we normally have a SCHD trade expiring nearly every month to bring up our cash flow, we'll be 'missing' out on September. Of course, for compensation for that, the premiums received are also higher due to the longer duration of the trade—meaning there is definitely a trade off.

Going out to October 17, 2025, is 57 days out. With a $0.33 premium collected on the short calls, we are looking at an annualized return of 7.55%. According to Fidelity, the probability that shares could finish above $28 is relatively high compared to most of our other trades.

For the short puts, we collected $0.45, which works out to an annualized potential return of 10.67%. However, for the short puts, these are cash-secured, and Fidelity is currently paying around 4% on idle cash in their money market (SPAXX). According to Fidelity, the probability that shares will be at $27 or below is fairly high, at ~33%.

When the fund goes ex-distribution, that will push it closer to that level. That should be why we are getting a bit more compensation on the downside when compared to the upside. Implied volatility is a bit higher, at 14.6% compared to 12.634% for the calls.

Conclusion

SCHD provides a higher dividend yield, while also providing solid dividend growth over the long term. The screening process includes some quality aspects, so they aren't just reaching for yield. This has led to its long track record of success. On the other hand, some will fault the fund for not keeping up with the S&P 500 Index—in my opinion, it is still accomplishing its main goal and providing respectable total returns.

On top of the healthy dividend on our long position, we've been generating additional cash flow nearly every month by writing options. Sometimes that is writing cash-secured puts or turning to covered calls—sometimes, it's even both, as we've recently done to set up a short strangle options position. It takes a bit more work for an investor, and it has its own pros and cons. However, it can be well worth it for investors to enhance their 'income' materially.

data.title: Qualcomm Remains A Buy Due To Edge AI Leadership
data.description:
It has been quite a while since I last covered Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM). Back in mid-February, I reiterated my buy rating on the stock, citing undervaluation and their strong position in edge AI. Since then, Qualcomm hasn't been on a particularly strong run, as declines are at around 10%.

In the below analysis, it is shown that fiscal Q3 results were generally quite respectable, but guidance for Q4 is very soft. While this may justify the low level in the forward P/E ratio, I believe with the company being a leader in edge AI, the current valuation offers opportunity to long-term investors that can wait out the consumer headwinds. Therefore, I reiterate my Buy rating on Qualcomm stock.

An Edge AI Leader

Before taking a look at their financial metrics, I would like to start with a brief discussion on some of their most important announcements in their FY2025 Q3. Firstly, the company announced an extension of their collaboration with Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF). They stated that Xiaomi will continue to use Snapdragon 8 for their premium phones and that the company "will be one of the first to adopt the next-generation premium Snapdragon 8-series to be announced later this year, in China and globally." Moving forward, Qualcomm and Xiaomi's collaboration could widen to more edge products like automotive and AR/VR. With Xiaomi being a major player in consumer electronics in China, I believe the continuation and deepening of their collaboration should further boost Qualcomm as one of the world's leaders in edge AI.

Another collaboration that I believe is worth mentioning is the one with Advantech that was announced at COMPUTEX 2025. Qualcomm stated that together they aim to "advance AI-driven IoT applications" and "accelerate] the deployment of intelligent solutions across industries." This shows that the company's edge ambitions are beyond just consumer products and that the market is larger than perhaps previously thought. In my view, these two major collaborations show off the company's desire to maintain their strong position in this space as edge AI becomes increasingly important.

Now, let's move on to their financial results.

Top Line Growth Is Slowing

Qualcomm reported their FY2025 Q3 earnings near the end of July, and top line results may be slightly worrying in my view. They generated a total of $10.37 billion in revenue, representing a 10.35% increase over the prior year period. Revenues dropped QoQ for the second straight quarter, and the YoY growth rate slowed for the third consecutive quarter. This shows that perhaps consumer weakness is weighing on demand for consumer electronics. Still, they were able to edge past expectations by $12.26 million, no pun intended. Their QCT segment was the leader for growth, as they saw a 11% increase in revenues versus their QTL segment's 4% increase.

Within their QCT segment, IoT growth remained quite strong vs. Q2 but handsets and automotive growth both slowed considerably as another hint that consumers may be cutting back on discretionary spending. As a whole, I would say that top line results show that despite long-term edge AI tailwinds, economic headwinds are taking a toll on the company.

Margins Still Expand

Despite the slowing of top-line growth, there were positive developments in Qualcomm's margins. For their QCT segment, they reported an EBT margin of 30%, an expansion from 27% in the year ago quarter, while for their QTL segment, they saw an increase from 70% to 71% YoY. As you can see in the above charts, both EBIT margin and net profit margin were notably higher than in the prior year period, and so there was an increase in profitability overall. Do note, however, that when you go and look at their income statement, it is shown that a notable contributor to this increase in margin is a decrease in R&D spending by 1.46% to $2.226 billion. While this causes near term gain, it may lower their long-term growth prospects, and so that's something investors should keep in mind.

For the quarter, Qualcomm reported an EPS of $2.77, up 18.88% YoY. This is a modest acceleration from Q1's 16.80% growth rate and far outpaces revenue growth due to the margin expansion. This EPS figure also beat consensus expectations by a narrow margin of $0.06 to show slight outperformance. Overall, profitability of the business was quite robust, especially considering that demand seems to be a concern.

Buybacks Soar

As shown above, Qualcomm's cash generation was a bit weak even as EPS growth was strong. They reported operating cash flows of $2.875 billion, representing a 5.8% decline. Despite this, shareholders should have been happy that capital returns increased significantly, mainly as a result of share repurchases. Total dividend payout increased from $949 million to $968 million while buybacks soared from $1.585 billion to $3.118 billion. Considering that the stock's dividend yield is currently 2.28%, if Qualcomm continues to buyback stock at this rate, shareholders could see their returns swell. With cash generation not being particularly strong, it could be hard to keep buying back stock at this pace moving forward, however.

Q4 Guidance Is Soft

Qualcomm provided guidance for their FY2025 Q4. For revenues, they expect a total of between $10.3 billion and $11.1 billion. At the midpoint, this implies a YoY growth rate of just 4.45%. This is a slowdown from Q3's 10.35% growth rate and would also represent the fourth consecutive slowdown in YoY growth. Clearly, economic headwinds are expected to continue pressuring consumer demand for discretionary electronics. For adjusted EPS, they are expecting a range of $2.75 to $2.95. At the midpoint, this would imply a 5.95% increase YoY. This is a sharp slowdown from Q3's 18.88% growth rate. Furthermore, the fact that EPS growth is not expected to outpace revenue growth by much shows that the margin improvements that I discussed earlier aren't expected to continue happening in their Q4. As a whole, guidance for Q4 was very soft and shows that the outlook is rather unfavorable.

Valuation May Be Attractive

The forward P/E ratio for Qualcomm sits at a low-level relative to the past couple of years. The current reading of 13.05 in the multiple potentially provides opportunity in my view. For Q3, EPS results were quite strong as growth accelerated. However, as discussed just a moment ago, the outlook for the company looks rather concerning, as growth is expected to drop sharply. While this may justify a low level in the forward P/E, I believe for investors that have faith in the long-term potential of the company, this valuation could be attractive.

Recall that I started off this analysis with a discussion on their partnerships with Xiaomi and Advantech. Through these collaborations and other innovative efforts, Qualcomm is very likely to remain a leader in edge AI. The weak near-term guidance is likely merely a reflection of poor consumer confidence, and so in the long term, I expect the company to be an important piece of the AI industry.

With that in mind, note that the IT sector's GAAP and non-GAAP forward P/Es are 28.99 and 23.21, respectively. Therefore, Qualcomm is at a massive discount to the sector, and the valuation offers opportunity for long-term investors that believe the company is going to grow with the edge AI market.

Takeaway

In QCOM's fiscal Q3 results, there were some signs of consumer weakness in the form of slowing top line growth. However, an expansion of margin meant that profitability was strong. In their Q4 guidance, it was shown that the business environment is continuing to deteriorate with a sharp EPS growth slowdown expected. While this may justify a depressed level in the forward P/E ratio, as stated above, I believe the company's collaborations and AI advancements are likely to keep them in a leadership position for edge AI. With that being the case, I expect the valuation to recover once economic headwinds dissipate, and so for long-term investors, this may be an attractive entry point. As a result, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Qualcomm stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
